content:
10 min read
Just now

--

What now?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Steve A Johnson on UnsplashI had to unfollow and unsubscribe from a creator I loved the other day.
When I first met them, when my eyes, ears, and heart first caught their words, their commentary, I was hooked. I didn’t want to let go of it. So for months at a time, I would consume their writing. It was so ingrained in me that I literally saw myself looking at headlines and wondering what such and such would say about this.
And I stepped away, their words dodged my algorithm. All of a sudden, I was reacquainted with them; I was reacquainted with their work in spurts. Things were different, things were new. They abandoned the Unsplash images for AI-generated ones that seemed to fit the discussion. I wasn’t bothered, the words were still there, they felt still reachable to me, and I to them. Until they weren’t.
I don’t know, something just changed. I stepped away, and my mind threw the image of an article that I read of theirs that moved me, gave me a deep word that I could carry with me. I wanted to recapture that feeling again with another topic, but I couldn’t because their words were different.
Their arguments were logically sound.
Their essays were short and straight to the point.
I just couldn’t get a feeling of what exactly was it that was offputing about this essay I agreed with in practice.
“That’s not such and such. That’s such and such in blank.”
Ahh. That’s not yours; you’ve never framed such a contrast like that before. When did you start? Why did you start? Why don’t you bring back those clumsy, those angry and aggressive analogies and stories, they were you in all the right places. I thought long and hard about where I saw that framing before. I saw it from other bigger creators; maybe this one was trying to copy.
I saw it in A.I., when I used to work with students writing, I would see that sentence too, a clean and easy way to show an undesirable difference. And it dawned on me maybe, this writer I was interacting with was using A.I. themselves, maybe, I don’t know.
So I left, not because I am against A.I. usage in its entirety. But, because I no longer knew the voice, I felt betrayed as a reader, that I was thought of as too “stupid and dumb” to spot the difference in tone, in analogy, in flow to acknowledge something had shifted. So I left that small orbit of a community. I couldn’t spot the difference anymore, and wouldn’t subject the piece to constant debate of " Is this real, or is this A.I?” I have too much respect for writing the science and the art form to do that.
I don’t want it to be wasted on you, the courage it takes to write a piece like this. Maybe now people are looking over their shoulders, maybe some more who I follow do use A.I. for their own reasons. I don’t need to hear them. I don’t intend to conjure up the desire to hear how hard life is where writing and research take too much time. Or how difficult it is for the left hand and the right hand to align to pen something meaningful. This is not what this piece is for. Not accusatory. I don’t come in peace, but I also am not hoping to start a war either.
I want to have a conversation with you all, which may force people to get caught on the edges of feeling critiqued. A.I. is not the problem; we are just walking down the road of symptoms that are sickening what we do here on this platform, on our blogs, on Substack, and dare I say, is even infecting the rungs of prestigious writing.
Come hear my thoughts.

Art vs Content, Production vs. Creation
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Birmingham Museums Trust on UnsplashYou can be prodigal. Productive. Able to create beautiful things.
But when that beauty becomes a process to appease markets, fans, or even court them, we are no longer speaking of art or creation. Creation for something tangible, such as these modes of literal or social currency, isn’t art any longer, but content.
When you have a message on your heart that you pen or paint, or sculpt, and you can say, I don’t care if anyone loves it, it is important and needs to be said; that is art.
However, we have entered an age where, to appease algorithms, audiences, and strangers we swear are friends, we produce things, create a “routine” to speak on the things that will give us favor and mitigate blowback. To keep the checks and applause rolling, but diminish conversations.
Content is the goal; maybe when you were smaller, it was to be seen, but you have your analytics now. You see the comments, you know exactly who and what people want to see to consume. So you keep churning out the same kind of content. You become less sharp or creative. Because creativity is easy in a room nobody sees, it’s easy in a world that doesn’t notice you.
Then get a following. Then continue churning and churning. Then speak on the same themes others wrote about, not providing nuance or clarity on how your mind works, but just content to appease the algorithm and say, I am worthy of your attention.
Content makes us feel comfortable, and it is easier to brush aside genuine critique, thought, and perspective as a person “just not getting it” or being out of the loop of the community.
We trip and fall over these discussions.
“So artists can’t make content? Or earn money?”
“A content creator cannot make art?”
Intent is the thermostat, not necessarily solely capital. Intent rules all and gains the most prevalence. And A.I. is the perfect conveyor belt for production, short-hand sayings, analogies generated not from sitting and grasping ideas, but plugging in prompts.
If all of this is true, that conveyor belt, that automation creates another more disturbing conundrum.

Automation of Our Thoughts
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Homa Appliances on UnsplashNo longer do you sit on an issue.
No longer do you allow the time-consuming moments to breed inspiration.
You allow the artificial intelligence to do the laborious part of writing. The processing and stewing give you a new perspective.
“I don’t have time.”
This is where that statement comes from. The idea that this effort of staying true to the process in search of gold isn’t worth the struggle, so you automate. You have A.I. essentially begin to automate your thoughts on an issue.
And because you have fully stepped into and embraced the role and idea of content over art, you don’t see that you have traded process for a momentary surface-level efficiency. You offload cognitively, in the name of a better product. A product that may leave someone like me wondering where the writer was in the piece we just read. And sometimes we, as readers, can be so self-hating.
We want so desperately to have our opinions, our ideas centered, but we don’t check the source of those opinions. We don’t extend ourselves to be validated or challenge ourselves to find work that resonates, and that feels.
“At least someone is telling it like it is.”
Yes, someone is.

Conundrum of (Dis)agreement
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Vitaly Gariev on UnsplashI’ve found myself in a weird place. Some writers, to make things down to earth and grounded, here on this platform, I disagree with. From an artistic or writer’s perspective, if you will.
They have their opinions, but it's built on faulty premises. Their foundation is just their personal anecdotes that the reader is forced to stand in goodwill and accept, because that is their research. No evidence, no data, no other anecdotes to corroborate what has been said. Just their own perspective and their own experience. If they took it a step further, did their own analyses from the observation as opposed to feeling the observation was a strong enough analysis, they may have come forth with a different argument or more nuanced perspective.
And honestly, out of love for this art form, I at times will let them slide by. Why? Because at least I know you formulated that opinion, no matter how wrong it is, on your own. You didn’t poll your followers first, you didn’t generate drafts on GPT and store them in a Word Doc. You took your opinions and fell on your own sword.
I feel placed in limbo, with that creator, and even others, who I agree with but whose words rhyme to others. Or their flow of sentence structure mirrors another creator; they don’t even follow.
When everything happens, you find yourself elevating work that misses the mark, because at least it’s real. When you are lied to for so long, you stomach when someone is heinous and brutal with their honesty. Because at least they tell the truth or some truth. Or their truth.

What if…
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Kaitlyn Baker on UnsplashWhat if your favorite writer uses A.I?
Will you abandon them like I did?
Will you keep going along for the ride just because they center your doubts, your fears?
These are questions you have to answer for yourself. Because I had to.
I love this thing we call writing; it is something that has given me opportunities that a ball or a boxing glove, or money, or a rich parent, or connections couldn’t give me. You can build worlds, you can rewrite your story, and describe yourself as the victor, not the victim.
You can heal wounds that not even a band-aid or aloe vera can touch. And it hurts me when some of my students came in and they GPTed their essays. Because they had a game, or they partied too hard and close to their due date, or their professor/teacher is really hard, or they need to pass the course. Not because it is just disrespectful to me, or to that teacher, but it is disrespectful to themselves. They don’t trust themselves to learn, to respect, or even to ask for help. So they cut through, they make content.
And then, I come here, or Substack, and feel the same syndrome running underneath, guiding those actions. Only this time, it isn’t people who need a grade or need to appease parents or what have you. It’s people who claim to want to be here, who have something to say. I don’t know which of the two is the bigger tragedy. The student who wants to automate his mind for a grade or the creator who does the same thing for likes and views?
I’m not judging anyone. But I will draw a line in the sand. I will tell you all what I told them.
“If you, by chance, feel you need AI. You must only use it for formatting, for outlines. Not taking your words and placing them on a page, then hitting send. Whether you just copy and paste, or you phrase from what AI told you, you must do this on your own, with our help, of course.”
I’m serious about this. It’s not about the money. It’s not about the views. I remember writing Mike Tyson, Jake Paul, and the State of Aging Men in America. I remember my only pieces being read by my girlfriend at the time, when I would send her my drafts.
I remember when Empathy Should Be Children’s First and Constant Lesson didn’t make a splash, but I loved it, not because I wrote it but because I had something to say.
I can’t tell you how much I love writing. And hurts, sometimes, but it’s necessary.
I don’t care about the debate, of which word Grammarly gave you. I don’t wanna know what hour of the day it was for you, where you thought you could get by copying that sentence.
I just want to know, where did your heart break a second time when you spoke about your father in that essay?
When you wrote about that marriage that didn’t work, what line can I, as the reader, see that you forgot you weren’t married anymore? I can picture when you cried tears of joy, and you tasted the flavor of that wedding cake, before you realized where you were.
I’m conflicted, because I want to hold space for you. But deep, I feel simultaneously, if I can’t find these things, if I can’t feel these things, if I can’t breathe in the exhaust of your pain with you, I-I don’t know if I can stand behind you and support, and give you a minute or an additional “1” on the view count o
